hook: Red-walled fortress from the town's 1471 founding, now a museum and Andalusian garden
facts: Built by Moulay Ali ben Rachid around 1471, the year he founded the town for Moorish and Jewish refugees from Spain
Ochre ramparts and a garden that contrast deliberately with the surrounding blue medina
Left wing holds an ethnographic museum opened in 1985: textiles, pottery, instruments, weapons
Held the Riffian rebel Abd el-Krim as a prisoner in the 1920s
w: Moulay Ali ben Rachid raised these red ramparts around 1471, the year he founded the town for Muslims and Jews fleeing reconquered Spain. The ochre walls and shaded Andalusian garden sit pointedly against the blue medina. A dungeon once held the Rif rebel Abd el-Krim. Now textiles and pottery in the keep.

hook: The cobbled main square laid out in the 16th century, named for a vanished bathhouse
facts: Probably created by Moulay Muhammad, a son of Ali ben Rachid, in the 16th century
Named for a hammam, a public bathhouse, that once stood in its northwest corner
Flanked by the red Kasbah and the Grand Mosque with its octagonal minaret
Once a training ground for riders before becoming the social heart of the town
w: Laid out in the 16th century, supposedly by a son of the town's founder, and named for a hammam that once steamed in its northwest corner. Riders trained here before the cafes moved in. The red Kasbah on one side, the octagonal minaret on the other, mint tea everywhere between. The medina's beating heart.

hook: The town's oldest mosque, crowned by a rare octagonal minaret
facts: Founded in the earliest period of the town, with sources citing 1471 or shortly after
Octagonal minaret thought to date from the late 17th century, decorated in three tiers of blind arches and zellij
Minaret style echoes those built under Ali ibn Abdallah Errifi, governor of Tangier
Closed to non-Muslims, like all working Moroccan mosques
w: Founded with the town itself around 1471, the oldest Friday mosque in Chaouen. The oddity sits on top: an octagonal minaret, rare in Morocco, banded in three tiers of blind arches and zellij and probably raised in the late 1600s. The style nods to Tangier's governor-builders. Locked to outsiders, admired from below.

a: Jebel hillside, east of the medina
hook: Hilltop colonial mosque that never held a prayer, now the sunset lookout
facts: Built by the Spanish in the 1920s during their occupation of northern Morocco
Rejected by locals as too foreign and never used for worship
Reached by a steep 20 to 25 minute climb east of the medina
Now the most popular vantage point over the blue town at sunset
w: The Spanish built it on the hill in the 1920s, a mosque in their own idea of Moorish style. The townsfolk found it foreign and never once prayed in it, so it stood empty for a century. Climb twenty steep minutes and it pays off anyway: the whole blue bowl of Chaouen below, best at sundown. Faith failed, the view won.

hook: The mountain spring where women still come to wash by hand
facts: Name means head of water or source of water in Arabic
Spring bursts from the Rif just beyond the medina's far northeastern gate
Channelled through stonework attributed to the town's Andalusian founders
Women still gather to wash fabrics here, a living centuries-old routine
w: Head of water, the Arabic name says, and it earns it: the spring crashes straight out of the Rif at the medina's far gate, channelled through stone the Andalusian founders laid. Women still kneel here scrubbing washing in the cold rush, then string it on the lines. Tourists arrive, the laundry carries on regardless.

hook: The keyhole gate that has guarded the medina since the 15th century
facts: Sandstone gate built during the reign of the town's founder, 1471 to 1511
Name translates as eye gate; guards once watched the medina from its upper storey
Another reading ties the name to a spring, ayn, that flowed nearby
Marks the line between the old fortified medina and the newer Spanish-era town
legend: Some say guards kept an eye on the medina from the upper chamber, giving the eye gate its name
w: Beige sandstone arch pierced through the wall in the founder's own century, 1471 to 1511. The name means eye gate: some say guards watched the lanes from the chamber above, others that a spring, an ayn, once flowed past. Step through and the Spanish town gives way to the old medina, daylight to shadow.

hook: A tiny working leather tannery tucked by the river, far from the Fez crowds
facts: Small artisan tannery in the medina near the Ras El Maa spring
Stone vats still cure and dye hides by hand using old methods
A modest counterpart to the vast tanneries of Fez, with almost no tourists
Sits beside the river that powers the town's washing and craft
w: Everyone photographs the dye pits of Fez; almost nobody finds this one. A handful of stone vats near the river above Ras El Maa, where a few tanners still cure and colour hides the slow way. The smell announces it before the alley does. No ticket booth, no terrace of touts. Just leather, lime and running water.

a: Between the Kasbah and Bab el-Ain
hook: The old Jewish quarter whose dye may have turned the whole town blue
facts: Lies between the Kasbah and Bab el-Ain, off Rue Moulay Ali Ben Rachid
Earliest buildings date to the 16th century in an Andalusian style brought by Spanish Jews
Known as Mellah el-Jedid, the new mellah, after Jews moved inside the walls in the 18th century
The last Jewish family left for Israel in 1968
legend: Legend ties the town's blue to a Jewish tradition of tekhelet dye recalling the heavens; rival tales credit mosquito-repelling or simply keeping houses cool
w: Tucked between Kasbah and Bab el-Ain, the quarter Spanish Jews built in Andalusian style, courtyards and tiled roofs. They called it the new mellah only after an 18th-century sultan walled them in. The last family sailed for Israel in 1968. Legend says their heaven-blue dye washed across the whole town; others blame mosquitoes.

hook: The makhzen square just outside the gate, once the seat of governing authority
facts: Open square just beyond Bab el-Ain, on the edge of the old medina
Name derives from makhzen, the term for the central governing authority in Morocco
Sits at the threshold between the fortified medina and the Spanish-era new town
Lined with cafes and the bustle of arriving visitors
w: Just outside the eye gate, the square that carries the heaviest word in Moroccan politics: makhzen, the governing power, the state itself. Here the fortified medina meets the newer Spanish town, and the name marks where authority once sat. Today it gathers cafes and the day's first arrivals. A doorstep between two cities.
